query: What did Bob Lazar claim about Element 115?

result.synthesis: Bob Lazar claimed that Element 115 (Moscovium) served as the fuel source for the alien spacecraft he allegedly worked on at the S4 facility near Area 51. According to Lazar, a stable isotope of Element 115 was used in an antimatter reactor that powered the craft's gravity propulsion system. He stated that when Element 115 was bombarded with protons, it transmuted into Element 116, which immediately decayed and released antimatter. This antimatter was then annihilated with matter in a controlled reaction to produce enormous energy. Lazar also claimed the US government possessed approximately 500 pounds of Element 115, obtained from an extraterrestrial source. Element 115 was not synthesized in a laboratory until 2003 at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna, Russia — 14 years after Lazar's initial claims.

research_plan.reason: Simple factual query — direct retrieval from corpus sufficient

corpus_results.content: Lazar described Element 115 as the key to the propulsion system. He explained that a disc of Element 115 was placed inside a reactor, where it was bombarded with protons. This transmuted the Element 115 into Element 116, which immediately decayed and in the process released antimatter. The antimatter was directed down a tuned tube and reacted with a gaseous matter target, producing complete annihilation and a massive release of energy.
corpus_results.excerpt: Lazar described Element 115 as the key to the propulsion system...

corpus_results.content: In the 2019 Joe Rogan interview, Lazar reiterated his claims about Element 115, stating he had personally handled a disc of the material and that the government possessed approximately 500 pounds of it. He described the material as orange in color with unusual properties, including the ability to generate its own gravitational field when properly machined and stimulated.
corpus_results.excerpt: In the 2019 Joe Rogan interview, Lazar reiterated his claims about Element 115...

corpus_results.content: Element 115, later named Moscovium, was first synthesized on February 2, 2003, by a joint team of Russian and American scientists at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) in Dubna, Russia. The synthesized atoms decayed in less than a second, unlike the stable isotope Lazar described. Critics note that while Lazar's prediction of Element 115's existence was notable, the properties he described do not match those observed in laboratory synthesis.
corpus_results.excerpt: Element 115, later named Moscovium, was first synthesized on February 2, 2003...
